speaker: interviewer
text: David Lipstadt, [? lot ?] four. David Lipstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would-- just wanted to ask you really quickly, when you were in hiding in-- for all those years-- did you keep a celebration or a mental note of, like, Jewish holidays? Or was it important? Did you know when they were coming around, and did you celebrate at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. We took notice of-- of them. But we didn't do anything. See, we were so out of place. Everything was out the place. And everything was-- doesn't matter, because you don't know whether you're gonna survive to another holiday. So we talked about it. And we regretted the fact that this is Jewish holiday and we are immune from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because your life was in such disarray? What about after the war-- did you resume--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But they didn't-- they didn't have the same flavor anymore. Because you didn't have the family, didn't have the friends. You didn't have the-- the proper environment to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How important was your Judaism to you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean as a religion?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my faith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To illustrated, [INAUDIBLE]-- to give you the proper answer, I can illustrate it with this fact. When I-- I used to go out from the ghetto every day and come back at night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Usually, we would-- we would buy some food on the Aryan side and bring it into the ghetto. We had to smuggle it into the ghetto. Because this-- it depended on the whim of the gendarme whether to let you through with this miserable little food you were trying to smuggle in and not. So I was very lucky. I would always-- I could somehow-- I could smuggle it in without being-- getting caught. The daily routine was as follows. I would take a tablecloth or a couple of pillow cases with me in the morning and meet this fellow who would be my liaison with the outside world. I would tell him, I would like a big piece of cheesecake and a couple of pears. For this, I gave him the tablecloth and the pillow cases and anything else, maybe a shirt. And he would bring it to me. Of course, tomorrow, he will get another order. You see, life was so meaningless we were sitting on the railroad tracks, dirty hands, dirty all over, and eat the best cheesecake available at the time from the best bakery, and eating the best pears available. Because we didn't-- every minute was-- was thought of as the last, could have been the last. So we didn't-- we didn't-- nothing-- nothing impressed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he able to get it to you without your getting caught? How were you not caught when he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, getting him? You had-- you had to hide it. Either you had a long coat with a pocket inside and he would hide things in there--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you [? would ?] [? go ?] marching in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe it or not-- it comes to my mind now-- once, I smuggled in a live carp, a fish--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the way back, this fellow told me he has fish if I want fish. So I says, yes. So on the way home, he joined the group. Not-- he didn't go inside the ghetto, but just enough for him to hand me the carp. And I hid it. And I went through the-- [LAUGHS] different episode come to my mind. I told you about this when they made the selection, when from the group of almost 300, they cut it down to 136. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We spent the day there surrounded by the SS. And after all the-- after they-- they selected their 136 people to-- to-- to live for a little more, I-- I was sitting there on the-- on the floor, on the sidewalk, before the gate, wait-- waiting for the gendarmes to tell us to move on. And they had reflectors facing us on the streets from the gate towards us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were five gendarmes. And five people from the group would approach everyone. And he would search them. Every gendarme would search one person, yeah? And-- and I was looking the way the gendarmes were working. And I saw one was very, very effective. He would look for diamonds or something for [INAUDIBLE] here. He would-- he didn't look for anything big. He thought something small might be-- good things come in small-- Packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: --packages. And I had to face this fellow. But next to him, was an older gendarme, a short fellow. And he was-- he wasn't as mean as this guy. So I said to a friend of mine, a worker-- coworker-- I said, do you have anything to-- to hide? He said, no. I have nothing. So I said, do you mind changing places? So he says, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we change places. This [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gendarme was so proficient, I would say, he said to me, come, come, come, come. [GERMAN]. You are in a hurry? Come, come, come. And I was going to him. While I was going to him, I remembered-- well, I remembered all the time. But it-- it appears to me that, I had a-- in my backpack, I had a metal box with watches, which my father gave me. He says, you are a boy. You might survive on those. They might help you out. And I had them in my rucksack. And I was facing this gendarme. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he calls me. When he called me, come, come. You're in a hurry? So I had to go. And like, I don't know, it was a miracle happened. But the Jewish policeman came over to him, and said-- saluted, and-- and said to him, there's a telephone call for you. And he moved back to answer the call. So I quickly went to this short fellow. And he asked me, what do you have-- what do you have to declare? What do you have to hide? So I-- I-- I had some coins, which they liked very much. Those were silver coins, which were issued right before the war. There was one with a ship, which was very rare. There was one with Pilsudski, which was a big hero in Poland. So I told him I have this ship. I have the 5 zloty with Pilsudski. I gave it to him. He took it. He accepted that and let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I passed past the gate and set down on the floor-- on the ground-- on the pavement. And took off-- I had a-- a very colorful scarf. I took this off, and I put it behind in-- in my jacket. And I was sitting there, hiding my face. And sure enough, he went by with a flashlight and was looking for me. He was looking specifically for me. But he couldn't find me, somehow. And I passed through with those watches.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was looking for you because he knew you had more coins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. He knew that I had something to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was one of the lucky moments.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know how you got the live carp through.

speaker: interviewee
text: I smuggled in butter too. And my-- my-- my brother-in-law, Mala's husband, had a single loaf of bread. And they took it away from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to him once they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was just lucky. I don't know what I did. But I did it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you smuggle it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked you earlier-- and you-- you sort of touched upon it. You said you've lost your faith.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Well, this-- this-- this is-- I used to pray every day. I was-- I was religious. I was brought up-- and if I missed a prayer, I-- I didn't feel right. One day, when the hunger in the air was unbelievable. You couldn't-- you couldn't-- you couldn't buy a live cat. There was nothing to eat at all. So I decided to bring in a piece of lard into the ghetto. I smuggled it in. I brought it home. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother looked at it and said, as long as I'm alive, we're not gonna eat it. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Three days later, my mother was taken away. And the lard was still there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this turned me around a little. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] How about today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I do the traditions. I observe all the tradition. But the faith is gone. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Your question-- you-- you ask questions, and you can find any answers. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you want future generations to know, who watch this tape? What would you like to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like the future generations to-- to know our history, that we are-- that we lived, in spite of all the obstacles, of all the haters. We are still alive. We are still a-- a people. And to those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who say that this never happened, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would like to find somebody to tell it to me, that this didn't happen. Because I was there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I lived through it. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would tell them that the world is beautiful. There's room for everybody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we could all do better by loving, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] instead of hating.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is there anything else that you want to add before we look at some pictures and meet your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my father, Hersch Lipstadt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He comes from a very religious family. And the hat-- the cap he has on was worn by religious people, by Orthodox Jews. But my father used to love the opera very much. And he would tell me-- he would take off this cap, hide it in his coat, and walk into the opera-- opera house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture-- picture was taken in Warsaw, where he was born and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was-- when was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's about how old there?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Aunt Gutta, Gutta Weisblundt. She's my mother's sister. She was born in Poland, in Warsaw. And she died while trying to check out the store during the bombardment of Warsaw in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1937. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my sister Lilka with me in a park. It was taken in 1936, when she was eight years old. I loved her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm David Lipstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you there in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 80 years old. She was-- in '36, I was 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She-- she was very active in the ghetto. She was a courier. She was bringing messages in from one group to the other. And she was very active, at such a young age, in organizing schools-- illegal schools, I might say. Because it was forbidden to have any legitimate schools in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was only eight years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was eight years old. This is my wife, Bala Lipstadt, and me, David Lipstadt, in Berlin in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my wife, Bala, and our daughter Helena. And the picture was taken in Berlin when we lived in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: The fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1947. We lived in Berlin for five years before we emigrated to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1951, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lipstadt. This is my uncle, Shlomo Weissblutt, who emigrated to Palestine at the time in 1924, with his daughter, Rina, and his wife, Shlomit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Israel. He urged us not to come at the time. It was a bad-- very bad time in Israel. So we decided to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your mother's brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will have to look in the back. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my nephew's wedding day in Israel in 1977. And from left to right, the people are [? Rachel, ?] which is married to the gentleman on the right--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, is-- is [? Rachel the ?] one in the black and white?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right. And next is [? Bolec, ?] which is-- and then comes Fela, which are husband and wife. Behind Fela is David, David Lipstadt-- me. Then, there's Lisa [INAUDIBLE]. Bala Lipstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the purple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. And the newlyweds. And then comes [? Marilka-- ?] [? Marilka ?] [? Zamir. ?] And this is [? Shimon. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the newlyweds? Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry and Sharon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. Leventhal.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I almost forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned Fela. And she is the woman standing behind him. That's your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my grandson, Isaac Lipstadt. Looks like he's--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's my only and favorite grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: He looks like he's at home. OK. David, could introduce everybody for us? Introduce--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Edith. I am David Lipstadt. This is my daughter, Helena-- my favorite daughter-- and my favorite son, Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helena, let me start with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you told about your father's experiences? At what point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember being told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The community that we grew up in was mostly survivors. And I think I picked up on their experience more through osmosis than by being told. I remember, until I was an adult, actually talking to my parents about what their life had been like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: A survivor. What about you, Aaron? At what point did you realize what your parents had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I still realize what my parents have gone through. But I do remember-- I must've been about 14 or 15-- reading and getting some idea of what had gone on in Warsaw and-- and talking to him-- talking to him about that. And-- and-- because it was so complete a picture of-- of the war in Warsaw and because of their reaction to street names and things like that, I realized both the accuracy of the book and it gave me some idea of-- of what that life-- what that was like then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of questions did you have for your father? Did you ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very basic questions. What happened to his family? To our family? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was also-- it's also-- I think it's very clear that those conversations were very difficult for them. And it made me, certainly, hesitant to pursue a difficult line like that, a difficult area of questioning. Until pretty recently, until I've had children and realized, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should know so I can tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, I know that you have also been through your own experiences during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how important is it to tell future generations who watch this-- what-- what should they know? What would you want to tell people?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they should appreciate what they have right now, what we went through, and how hard it was. Since we ourselves cannot believe what we went through. And also, that's why it's very important that they should know everything what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, how can we prevent it from really happening again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only through education. By telling-- by-- by doing what you are doing for the future generations. What convinced me to go before the cameras was what [? Norma ?] said-- or did you say it? That 50 years from now, there will be no survivors. And people like [INAUDIBLE] and the others who would question-- not question, but deny-- the Holocaust ever happening-- if we won't do it now, if we won't educate Gentiles and Jews of what happened-- because there's a lot of people who don't even know that something like this happened. So in order to prevent this from happening again, we have to educate the people, talk about it, and-- and tell them and confront them with videos like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just going to ask you, quickly, how did it affect your family growing up, being children of survivors? Do you think it affected how you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in a big way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that I defined myself as a daughter of survivors for many years. That was a presenting fact about myself for a long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I don't know. I think there's a certain protectiveness in our family, a certain caution, also a lot of silence, I think. As Aaron said, we were afraid to ask. Because we didn't want to stir things up, even though we knew a lot through our skins.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, also, we grew up in a-- although a lot of my parents' friends were survivors, they weren't part of our immediate community. We grew up in the community where, I think, our Jewishness made us different and the foreignness of our parents made us different. And there was-- there wasn't much real assimilation with our intermediate neighborhood and community. And I think that was certainly defined by, you know, our parents' background, and-- and-- and certainly defines us to some extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. And also not having grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I guess we're going to wrap it up real quickly. Does anybody want to add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, can I add something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- after what I saw what David, you know, did now today, I make up my mind I will do it too. I had twice appointment, and I had to cancel in Yale, in Connecticut, to do the video, because I wasn't up to it. It was-- it's very painful. Because we never forget what we went through. And it's very hard to talk about it. But today, I decided I will do it soon as I come back to Connecticut. I just want to add it, because I have two daughters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My older daughter, she is active, you know, with child survivors. And we're very thankful what we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I hope you do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to thank my-- my daughter and my son. [CRYING] They were always inquisitive. They always ask questions. And they-- they try-- we would sit down at the table and, out of nowhere, they would ask a question [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and talk about it and keep talking. And-- and-- and they are very, very supportive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I love them [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
